prompt: Lisa's brother is called Bart. (Whose brother? → his/her)
hint: One owner → 'his/her' = suo / sua. It agrees with the NOUN, not the owner: 'fratello' is masculine singular → suo. Singular family members drop the article.
explanation: Suo fratello = his/her brother. The possessive matches the thing owned (fratello, m.sg) → suo, and singular family drops the article (not 'Il suo fratello').

prompt: Bart's mother is called Marge. (Whose mother? → his/her)
hint: One owner → suo / sua, agreeing with the noun. 'madre' is feminine singular → sua. Singular family → no article.
explanation: Sua madre = his/her mother. Agrees with madre (f.sg) → sua; singular family drops the article.

prompt: Lisa AND Bart's father is Homer. Two people share him → 'their'.
hint: Two or more owners → 'their' = loro. Loro never changes form, and (unlike the others) it ALWAYS keeps the article — even with family → Il loro.
explanation: Il loro padre = their father. Two owners → loro. Loro is invariable and always keeps the article: 'Il loro padre' ✓ (not 'Loro padre').

prompt: Bart's grandparents are Abraham and Mona. (Whose grandparents? → his)
hint: One owner → suo-. 'nonni' is masculine plural → suoi. PLURAL family keeps the article → I suoi.
explanation: I suoi nonni = his grandparents. Agrees with nonni (m.pl) → suoi; plural family keeps the article.

prompt: Bart's sister is called Lisa. (Whose sister? → his)
hint: One owner → suo / sua, agreeing with the noun. 'sorella' is feminine singular → sua. Singular family → no article.
explanation: Sua sorella = his sister. Agrees with sorella (f.sg) → sua; singular family drops the article.

prompt: Homer is Marge's husband. (Whose husband? → her)
hint: One owner → suo / sua, agreeing with the noun. 'marito' is masculine singular → su-. (In this drill marito is used WITH the article.) → il suo.
explanation: Il suo marito = her husband. Note: standard Italian usually DROPS the article with marito/moglie ('suo marito'), so check how your class does it.

prompt: Bart's aunts are Patty and Selma. (Whose aunts? → his)
hint: One owner → su-. 'zie' is feminine plural → sue. PLURAL family keeps the article → Le sue.
explanation: Le sue zie = his aunts. Agrees with zie (f.pl) → sue; plural family keeps the article.

prompt: Homer's grandfather is called Abraham. (Whose grandfather? → his)
hint: One owner → suo / sua. 'nonno' is masculine singular → suo. Singular family → no article.
explanation: Suo nonno = his grandfather. Agrees with nonno (m.sg) → suo; singular family drops the article.

prompt: Ling is Selma's (adopted) daughter. (Whose daughter? → her)
hint: One owner → suo / sua. 'figlia' is feminine singular → sua. Singular family → no article.
explanation: Sua figlia = her daughter. Agrees with figlia (f.sg) → sua; singular family drops the article.

prompt: Herb is Homer's brother. (Whose brother? → his)
hint: One owner → suo / sua. 'fratello' is masculine singular → suo. Singular family → no article.
explanation: Suo fratello = his brother. Agrees with fratello (m.sg) → suo; singular family drops the article.

prompt: Make it plural: 'la tua casa' (your house) → your houses.
hint: Everything goes feminine plural: article la→le, possessive tua→tue, noun casa→case.
explanation: le tue case = your houses. The article, possessive, and noun all agree: la tua casa → le tue case.

prompt: Make it plural: 'il mio amico' (my friend) → my friends.
explanation: i miei amici = my friends. mio → miei in the masculine plural.

prompt: Make it plural: 'il suo libro' (his/her book) → his/her books.
explanation: i suoi libri = his/her books. suo → suoi in the masculine plural.

prompt: Make it plural: 'il loro giornale' (their newspaper) → their newspapers.
hint: loro never changes — only the article and noun pluralize: il→i, giornale→giornali.
explanation: i loro giornali = their newspapers. loro is invariable; only il→i and giornale→giornali change.

prompt: Talking about your own bed: 'I always sleep in MY bed.'
hint: Owner = io (I) → mi-. 'letto' is masculine singular → mio.
explanation: nel mio letto = in my bed. io → mio, matching letto (m.sg).

prompt: Talking about your own mother — she's called Paola. (My mother)
hint: Owner = io → mi-. 'madre' is feminine singular → mia. Singular family → no article.
explanation: Mia madre = my mother. io → mia (f.sg); singular family drops the article (not 'La mia madre').

prompt: Talking about your own grandparents — they're very old. (My grandparents)
hint: Owner = io → mi-. 'nonni' is masculine plural → miei. PLURAL family keeps the article → I miei.
explanation: I miei nonni = my grandparents. io → miei (m.pl); plural family keeps the article.
